1: The soul-binding between Jonathan and David: wᵉnepeš yôhônātān niqšərāh bᵉnepeš dāwiḏ wayyeʾĕhāḇēhû yôhônātān kᵉnapšô — 'and the soul (nepeš) of Jonathan was knit (niqšərāh) to the soul of David, and Jonathan loved him as his own soul.' The verb qāšar (to bind, knit, tie) is the construction-verb: what happens is an ontological binding of two napšôt. The nepeš in Hebrew is not merely the 'soul' in a Platonic sense but the whole animated person — the breathing, feeling, willing self. Jonathan's self-gift to David (stripping his robe, armor, sword, bow, belt in v4) enacts in action what the soul-binding describes in ontological terms. The love standard — 'as his own soul' (kənapšô) — is the Leviticus 19:18 standard that Jesus cites as the second great commandment.
3: The covenant between Jonathan and David: wayyiḵrot yôhônātān wəḏāwiḏ bərît bəʾahăḇātô ʾōtô kənapšô — 'Jonathan cut a covenant with David, in his love for him as his own soul.' The kārat bərît (cutting a covenant) formula marks this as a formal covenant transaction, not merely friendship. The covenant is motivated by love (ʾahăḇāh) — the word used for covenant-loyalty love throughout the OT (Deuteronomy 6:5; 1 Samuel 7:9; 1 Kings 3:3). Jonathan's enactment of covenant love toward David — the crown prince stripping himself of his royal insignia to clothe the shepherd — is the OT's most vivid picture of kenotic self-emptying in covenant: rank, privilege, and weapon transferred to the beloved. The NT pattern is explicit: 'He who had equality with God did not count it a thing to be grasped but emptied himself' (Philippians 2:6-7).
5: David succeeds everywhere: wayyaśkēl dāwiḏ bᵉḵōl ʾăšer yišlāḥēhû šāʾûl — 'David acted wisely (śākal) in everything Saul sent him to do.' The verb śākal (in the hiph'il: to act wisely, to prosper through prudent action) is the vocabulary of covenant wisdom — the same root as haśkîl in Psalms 101:2 and maśkîl in the Psalm superscriptions. The verb combines wisdom, insight, and the resulting success: to act with discernment that leads to divine favor and practical effectiveness. The pattern — the Spirit-anointed son who succeeds in everything — runs from Joseph (Genesis 39:3 — 'YHWH caused all he did to succeed') through David to the messianic portrayal of the Servant who 'acts wisely' (yaśkîl) before his suffering (Isaiah 52:13 — 'my servant shall act wisely / be high and lifted up').
12: Saul's fear of David: wayyirāʾ šāʾûl mippənê dāwiḏ kî hāyāh YHWH ʿimmô ûmēʿim šāʾûl sār — 'Saul was afraid of David because YHWH was with him but had departed from Saul.' The structure is a covenant-reversal: YHWH ʿimmô (YHWH was with him) has transferred from Saul to David. The divine presence-formula (YHWH ʿim X) tracks covenant election: it is with Abraham (Genesis 21:22), Isaac (Genesis 26:28), Jacob (Genesis 28:15), Joseph (Genesis 39:2), Moses (Exodus 3:12), Joshua (Joshua 1:5), Gideon (Judges 6:12), and now David. Saul's fear is the inverse of covenant confidence — he sees that the divine presence that underwrites success has left him and rests on his rival. Saul's yārēʾ (fear) of David is the fear that comes from recognizing that YHWH has chosen another.
2: lōʾ nəṯānô lāšûḇ bêṯ ʾāḇîw — 'he did not let him return to his father's house.' Saul's retention of David after the battle formalises the transition hinted at in 1 Samuel 16:21–22. The verb nāṯan (to give, allow) in the negative implies royal fiat: David is now a court permanent rather than an occasional visitor. This new status simultaneously elevates David and removes him from his father's protection, setting up the later scenes of vulnerability.
4: wayyiṯpašəṭ yônāṯān ʾeṯ-hamməʿîl ʾăšer ʿālāyw wayyiṯtənēhû lədāwid — 'Jonathan stripped off the robe he was wearing and gave it to David.' The gift of the crown prince's robe (məʿîl) to David is symbolically loaded: the məʿîl is a garment of royal status (cf. 1 Samuel 15:27; 1 Samuel 24:5, 11, where it marks Saul's kingship). Jonathan's voluntary divestiture anticipates the transfer of the kingdom and is a de facto act of covenant abdication: 'your kingship will be established and I will be second to you' (1 Samuel 23:17).
6: wattēṣeʾnāh hanaššîm mikōl ʿārê yiśrāʾēl lāšîr wəhamməḥōlōṯ — 'women came out from all the towns of Israel to sing and to dance.' The women's victory celebration with tambourines (tuppîm), singing, and three-stringed instruments (šālîšîm) follows the Miriam-pattern of Exodus 15 — the women lead the victory song after the decisive battle. The public, communal nature of the celebration makes Saul's jealousy over the song-content all the more politically significant.
7: hikkāh šāʾûl ʾăḷāpāyw wədāwid bərəḇābotāyw — 'Saul has struck down his thousands, but David his ten-thousands.' The antiphonal couplet uses the standard Hebrew poetic number escalation: ʾălāpîm (thousands) vs. rəḇābôṯ (ten-thousands). In victory songs this is a device of comparative praise, not arithmetic; the women are singing in Saul's honour of the whole campaign. But Saul will read it as a zero-sum comparison (v8).
8: wayyiḥar ləšāʾûl məʾōḏ wayyēraʿ bəʿênāyw haddāḇār hazzeh — 'very angry... displeased him greatly' (lit. 'it was evil in his eyes'). rāʿ bəʿênāyw is the technical formula for moral and political displeasure (cf. 2 Samuel 11:27, where YHWH uses the same formula for David's sin). Saul's self-comparison — 'only the kingdom is left for him' (hāmalûḵāh ʿôḏ-lô) — is theologically ironic: this is precisely what Samuel has prophesied (1 Samuel 13:14; 1 Samuel 15:28). Saul's fear is accurate.
9: wayyəhî šāʾûl ʿôyen ʾeṯ-dāwid — 'Saul kept watching David with suspicion/an evil eye.' The phrase ʿôyen ʿayin (lit. 'eye-ing with the eye') implies hostile surveillance; some translations render 'eyed him with jealousy.' The continuous participle marks this as an ongoing attitude that will define the rest of Saul's relationship with David. What begins as jealousy (v8) becomes structural enmity (v29) becomes active murderous intent (1 Samuel 19:1).
10: wattislaḥ rûaḥ-ʾĕlōhîm rāʿāh ʾel-šāʾûl — 'an evil spirit from God rushed upon Saul.' The formulation rûaḥ-ʾĕlōhîm rāʿāh ('evil spirit from/of God') repeats the language of 1 Samuel 16:14–15 and raises the same theological question: in what sense does YHWH send the evil spirit? The standard reading is that YHWH permits a distressing spirit as part of the judicial abandonment of Saul after his rejection (1 Samuel 15:26–28); the spirit is called 'from God' because it operates within YHWH's sovereign governance.
11: wayyāṭel šāʾûl ʾeṯ-haḥănîṯ — 'Saul hurled the spear.' The verb ṭûl (to cast, hurl) here is used for the first of three spear-throwing incidents in Samuel (1 Samuel 18:11; 1 Samuel 19:10; 1 Samuel 20:33). The spear (ḥănîṯ) was a royal symbol — carried by Saul as a sceptre-like weapon (1 Samuel 22:6; 1 Samuel 26:7). Throwing it at David transfers the symbol from dominion to violence; wayyissōḇ dāwid mippānāyw paaʿmayim ('David eluded him twice') — the double escape foreshadows David's repeated deliverance.
13: wayyāsirēhû šāʾûl mēʿimmô wayyāśem ʾōṯô lô śar-ʾalep — 'Saul removed him from his personal service and made him a commander of a thousand.' The removal from ʿimmô ('with him,' personal attendance) to field command over a śar-ʾalep ('commander of a thousand,' a standard military unit) is a combination of lateral promotion and distancing. David is simultaneously honoured and removed from the potentially dangerous proximity of the court. The plan backfires: wayyēṣēʾ wayyābōʾ ('he went out and came in') and the people's love for him grows (v16).
14: wayhî dāwid maśkîl bəḵōl dərāḵāyw — 'David prospered/acted wisely in all his ways.' śāḵal (Hiphil: to be wise, prudent, to prosper) is a key term in the wisdom tradition; its use here ties David's success to divinely-given wisdom rather than military prowess alone. The causal clause wayhwh ʿimmô ('and YHWH was with him') repeats the theological explanation of David's success that will recur throughout Samuel (cf. 2 Samuel 5:10; 1 Samuel 7:3).
15: wayyirʾ šāʾûl ʾăšer hûʾ maśkîl məʾōḏ — 'Saul saw that he was very successful/wise.' Saul's fear (yārēʾ) is now directed at David's demonstrated competence, not just the women's song. The verb śāḵal recurs from v14: what David is (maśkîl) is exactly what Saul fears. Fear and awe of YHWH's chosen one is a recurring motif; Saul's fear of David is a perverse form of recognising the presence of YHWH with David.
16: wəḵol-yiśrāʾēl wîhûḏāh ʾōhēḇ ʾeṯ-dāwid — 'all Israel and Judah loved David.' The subject 'all Israel and Judah' reappears (cf. v5, 1 Samuel 17:52) as the narrative marker of David's national support cutting across the later tribal divide. yōṣēʾ wāḇāʾ lipnêhem ('going out and coming in before them') is the idiom for military leadership (Numbers 27:17; Deuteronomy 31:2); the people love him because he leads from the front. This is precisely what Saul was supposed to do (1 Samuel 8:20) and cannot.
17: hinnēh bittî hagəḏôlāh mēraḇ — 'here is my eldest daughter Merab.' Saul's offer of Merab fulfils the reward promised in 1 Samuel 17:25. raq heyēh-lî libben-ḥayil ('only be a courageous man for me') conditions the marriage on continued military service, effectively using Merab as an incentive to keep David fighting — and potentially dying — against the Philistines. The phrase 'let the hand of the Philistines be against him' (v17b) makes Saul's motive explicit.
18: mî ʾānōḵî ûmî ḥayyay — 'who am I, and what is my life?' David's self-deprecation before Saul is the correct ANE vassal speech — humility before the king. mišpaḥaṯ ʾāḇî bəyiśrāʾēl ('my father's family in Israel') — the question about his clan's status suggests David recognises that his family is not noble enough to provide a queen. The response delays the marriage while maintaining correct protocol; Saul uses the delay to give Merab elsewhere.
19: wattinnaṯēn mēraḇ baṯ-šāʾûl ləʿadrîʾēl hamməḥōlaṯî — 'Merab was given to Adriel the Meholathite.' The passive construction (tinnaṯēn) removes Saul from grammatical agency — suggesting either that Saul quietly arranged the betrothal before David could claim Merab, or that it happened without dramatic confrontation. Adriel from Abel Meholah (possibly Tell Abu Sifri in the Jordan valley) is otherwise unknown; the marriage is purely strategic for Saul, disposing of the promised bride without honouring the promise.
20: wattēʾahaḇ miḵal baṯ-šāʾûl ʾeṯ-dāwid wayyaggidû ləšāʾûl wayyiyšar haddāḇār bəʿênāyw — 'Michal... loved David and Saul was pleased.' The note that Michal loves David is the only OT passage where a woman's love for her husband is explicitly stated as initiating the marriage relationship. Saul's pleasure is sinister: he sees an opportunity to use Michal as a snare (môqēš, v21) since David's love for Michal will make him take greater risks.
21: ʾettənennāh lô wətihy-lô lāmôqēš — 'I will give her to him so that she may be a snare to him.' mōqēš (trap, snare) is the same word used in covenant warnings against intermarriage with Canaanites (Ex 1 Samuel 23:33; 1 Samuel 34:12). Saul is planning to use Israel's marriage institution as a weapon against YHWH's chosen man. The verb tihy-lô lāmôqēš ('she will be a snare to him') is a bitter inversion of Ruth 4:11, where a good wife is a blessing.
22: ʾitdabəru ʾel-dāwid bassēṯer — 'speak to David privately.' Saul instructs his servants to create false intimacy — telling David that Saul is 'pleased with him' and that 'his servants love him' — to pressure David into pursuing Michal. The word bassēṯer ('in secret/privately') signals that the whole transaction operates through deception. David's eventual response (v23) shows he sees through the social pressure without being deceived about his own status.
23: haniqallāh bəʿênêḵem hiṯḥatten bammelek — 'Does it seem a small thing to you to become the king's son-in-law?' David's response again uses the expected humility formula but also signals awareness of the political significance of the offer. ʾanōḵî ʾîš-rāʾš ûniqləh ('I am a poor and lightly-esteemed man') — rāʾš (poor, without resources) and niqləh (of light weight, held in low regard) are the social vocabulary of outsider status. David's stated self-assessment protects him from appearing to seek the throne.
24: wayyaggidû ləšāʾûl ʾēmōr kaddəḇārîm hāʾēlleh dibber dāwid — 'they reported back to Saul: this is what David said.' The servants' function as intermediaries creates a layer of mediation between Saul's manipulation and David's response; David never speaks directly to Saul in these marriage negotiations. The report structure allows Saul to proceed to his next manoeuvre (v25) without being directly refused.
25: ʾên ḥēpeṣ lammelek bemōhar kî bimêʾāh ʿarlôṯ pəlištîm — 'the king desires no dowry (mōhar) except a hundred foreskins of the Philistines.' The mōhar was the standard bride-price paid by the groom's family; Saul replaces it with a military trophies requirement. The mutilation of slain enemies (taking their foreskins or hands as body-count proof) was documented in ANE warfare; 2 Samuel 3:14 preserves the completed transaction as '200 foreskins.' Saul's explicit motive — 'as vengeance on the king's enemies' — frames the request as holy war service while actually intending David's death.
26: wayyîšar haddāḇār bəʿênê dāwid liḥtiḥatten bammelek — 'it pleased David to become the king's son-in-law.' The parallel phrasing to Saul's pleasure at Michal's love (v20, wayyiyšar haddāḇār bəʿênāyw) is deliberate irony: both king and subject are 'pleased,' but for entirely different reasons. David accepts the terms; the narrator notes that the time set was not yet expired — he will move before Saul expects.
27: wayyāqom dāwid wayyēlek hûʾ wəʾanāšāyw wayyakku ḇappəlištîm māʾtayim ʾîš — 'David and his men struck down two hundred Philistines.' David delivers double the required number (200 vs. 100), and the narrator records that he presented the foreskins in full count to the king. The doubling is not explained — perhaps military circumstance — but functions narratively as excess zeal that confounds Saul's expectation of David's death. Saul is forced to give Michal as promised, and the marriage is formalised.
28: wayyarʾ šāʾûl wayēḏaʿ kî yhwh ʿim-dāwid — 'Saul saw and knew that YHWH was with David.' Saul's knowledge is explicit recognition of the theological reality: the reason David cannot be killed is not his own skill but divine protection. ûmiḵal baṯ-šāʾûl ʾăhēḇāṯhû ('and Michal his daughter loved him') repeats Michal's love as political fact — the tool Saul intended as a snare has become evidence of David's irresistible favour.
29: wayyōsep šāʾûl lîrōʾ mippənê dāwid ʿôḏ wayhî šāʾûl ʾōyēḇ ʾeṯ-dāwid kol-hayyāmîm — 'Saul became even more afraid of David, and was David's enemy all his days.' The progression is complete: suspicion (v9) → fear (v12) → active enmity (ʾōyēḇ, enemy) for 'all his days.' kol-hayyāmîm ('all the days') is a permanent narrative verdict; the relationship between Saul and David is now defined as structural enmity, not episodic conflict.
30: wayyaśkēl dāwid mikkōl ʿaḇdê šāʾûl wayyiqar šəmô məʾōḏ — 'David performed more successfully than all Saul's servants, and his name was highly honoured.' The chapter ends on the twin notes that characterise David throughout 1 Samuel: śāḵal (wise/successful — repeated from vv5, 14, 15) and yāqar šēm ('his name was precious/highly esteemed'). Both terms are gifts of YHWH's presence; the chapter that began with Goliath's taunt ends with David's name held in high honour.
